title: Why the trigger of being complimented on strength activates grief
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
It never felt like a compliment 
 Someone tells you you're strong. Maybe they say it with admiration. Maybe they say it with concern. Maybe they just say...
htmlContent:
It never felt like a compliment

Someone tells you you're strong. Maybe they say it with admiration. Maybe they say it with concern. Maybe they just say it offhand, like it's obvious. And something in you crumples. Not visibly. Not in a way anyone would notice. But inside, something twists. A familiar ache rises up. Grief. That's what it is. Grief. And you don't know why.

I've been there. I've stood in front of someone who meant well, who saw my survival as something to praise, and felt my throat close. Felt tears I couldn't explain pressing behind my eyes. Felt a strange, hollow anger that made no sense. Because they were being kind, right? They were seeing me. Weren't they?

Here's the thing. When someone calls you strong, especially when you're still in the middle of surviving, they're not seeing you. They're seeing your armor. They're seeing the part of you that learned to endure. The part that learned to swallow pain and keep moving. And that part of you, the part that got you through unspeakable things, is also the part that has been completely alone. For years. Maybe for your whole life.

Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote about how trauma lives in the body, talks about this. He says that trauma survivors often develop a kind of hypervigilance, a constant scanning for danger, that gets mistaken for resilience. People see the functioning. They don't see the cost. They don't see the nervous system running on empty, the body bracing for the next blow, the mind that never gets to rest. They see the survival. They don't see the grief of having to survive at all.

So when someone says "you're so strong," what you actually hear is: "You had to go through that alone." "No one was there to catch you." "You learned to carry everything because no one else would carry it with you." And that's not a compliment. That's a reminder of a wound that never fully healed.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying people are malicious when they say it. Most of the time they're not. They're just looking at your life from the outside, seeing the results, not the process. But your body knows the difference. Your body remembers every time you had to be strong because there was no other option. Every time you swallowed your tears so you wouldn't burden someone else. Every time you told yourself "I'm fine" when you were drowning.

That's the grief that gets activated. It's not about the compliment itself. It's about what the compliment represents. It's about the fact that you had to become strong in the first place. And that's not something to celebrate. That's something to mourn.

The nervous system hears something different

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say that your nervous system is always listening. Not just to words, but to the safety cues underneath them. And when someone calls you strong, your nervous system might not hear admiration. It might hear a demand. It might hear: "You need to keep being strong." "Don't fall apart now." "I need you to be okay so I don't have to feel uncomfortable."

Think about it. When you were a kid, or when you were in that abusive relationship, or when you were going through that period of relentless loss, who was there to hold you while you fell apart? Probably no one. Probably you learned very quickly that your pain made other people uncomfortable. That your tears were too much. That your fear was a burden. So you learned to be strong. You learned to hold it together. And you learned that being strong was the only way to be loved, or at least the only way to not be abandoned.

So now, decades later, when someone praises your strength, your body goes right back to that place. It goes back to the child who had to be a grown-up. The teenager who had to parent their own parents. The adult who walked through hell and never let anyone see them bleed. And your body says: "They need me to be strong. They can't handle my real feelings. I have to keep this up forever."

That's exhausting. It's a lifetime of exhaustion compressed into a single moment. And grief is the only thing that can hold that much exhaustion.

I want to be clear about something. You are not broken because this happens. You are not ungrateful. You are not making too big a deal out of a simple compliment. Your nervous system is doing exactly what it was trained to do. It's protecting you from the possibility of being seen as weak, because being seen as weak once meant danger. It meant you might not survive. Your body doesn't know that the danger is past. It just knows the pattern. And it reacts.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma is stored in the body as incomplete survival responses. The fight, flight, or freeze that couldn't complete itself back then is still sitting in your tissues. So when something triggers that old pattern, like a compliment on your strength, your body goes into that unfinished response. The grief is the energy of all those moments you couldn't cry, couldn't scream, couldn't collapse. It's the energy of survival finally getting to rest, even for a second.

Why the grief feels so big

Here's what I've learned from my own recovery and from talking to other survivors. The grief that comes up when someone calls you strong is not just about one moment. It's about every moment. It's the cumulative weight of every time you had to be the strong one. Every time you had to comfort someone who should have been comforting you. Every time you had to figure it out alone. Every time you had to smile when you wanted to scream.

It's the grief of a childhood you never got to have. The grief of relationships where you gave everything and got crumbs. The grief of a body that learned to hold tension instead of receiving care. The grief of a nervous system that never learned how to rest because rest was never safe.

That's a lot of grief. And it doesn't fit neatly into a five-minute cry. It's the kind of grief that comes in waves, sometimes for years. It's the kind of grief that makes you wonder if you'll ever feel anything other than tired.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). That book helped me name what I'd been carrying for so long. It helped me see that the grief wasn't a sign of weakness. It was a sign that I'd been running on fumes for decades.

And look, I know the wellness industry loves to tell you that you need to "reframe" your thoughts. That you need to see the compliment as a gift. That you need to "choose" a different response. Honestly, I think that's garbage. Not because reframing is always wrong, but because it skips the most important step. You have to let the grief come first. You have to let your body feel what it's been holding. You can't bypass the pain with positive thinking. That's just another form of being strong. That's just another way of telling yourself to shut up and keep going.

So no, I'm not going to tell you to be grateful for the compliment. I'm going to tell you to let yourself feel the grief when it comes. Let yourself cry if you need to. Let yourself be angry if that's what's there. Let yourself say, "I didn't want to be strong. I wanted to be held."

What your nervous system actually needs

When the grief hits, your nervous system is telling you something. It's telling you that it's tired of performing. It's telling you that it wants to be seen for real, not just for the survival act. It's telling you that it needs safety, not more demands.

So what do you do with that? How do you respond when the trigger happens?

First, you stop. You don't have to explain yourself to the person who gave the compliment. You don't have to correct them. You don't have to say anything at all. You just notice what's happening in your body. Notice the tightness in your chest. The lump in your throat. The heaviness in your limbs. Notice it without trying to change it. Just let it be there.

Second, you breathe. Not in some elaborate pattern that requires a PhD. Just breathe. Slow down your exhale. Let your belly soften. Let your shoulders drop if they can. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It's simple, but it works. It helps your vagus nerve get the message that you're safe now.

Third, you give yourself permission to not be strong. Just for a minute. Just for an hour. Just for today. You let yourself be the person who doesn't have to hold it together. You let yourself be the person who gets to fall apart if that's what's needed. You let yourself be the person who gets to receive, instead of always giving.

I know that sounds terrifying. I know that being weak, or even just not-strong, might feel like a death sentence. But here's the truth. Your strength is not what makes you lovable. Your survival skills are not what make you worthy. You were worthy before you ever had to be strong. You were worthy as a helpless baby who couldn't do anything for themselves. That's still true now.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It helped me understand why my body reacted the way it did, and why the grief was so physical. It's not an easy read, but it's a validating one.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The weight can help your body feel held in a way it maybe never did before.

This is not a problem to solve

I want to be honest with you. This grief, the one that gets triggered by being called strong, it's not something you're going to fix. It's not something you're going to get over. It's something you're going to learn to hold. It's something you're going to learn to let move through you, like weather passing through a .

Because here's what I've found. The more I tried to stop feeling this grief, the bigger it got. The more I tried to be grateful for the compliments, the more hollow I felt. The more I tried to "heal" myself into not being triggered, the more I was just doing the same thing I'd always done: trying to be strong enough to not feel my feelings.

So maybe the work isn't about not being triggered. Maybe the work is about letting the trigger show you where you're still hurting. Maybe the work is about letting yourself grieve, over and over, until the grief doesn't feel like it's going to drown you. Maybe the work is about telling the truth, even if only to yourself: "I am so tired of being strong. And I don't know who I am without it."

That's not a neat ending. I don't have one for you. I don't have five steps to make this stop. What I have is permission. Permission to feel the grief. Permission to not be okay with the compliment. Permission to be exactly where you are, even if that place is raw and tender and confusing.

You are not alone in this. Your nervous system is not broken. Your grief is not a mistake. It's the part of you that still knows what you lost. It's the part of you that still wants to be held. And that part of you, the one that never got what it needed, deserves your compassion. Not your strength.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Running on Empty (paid link) - For understanding emotional neglect and the grief of unmet needs.
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - For understanding how trauma lives in the body and nervous system.
  • Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link) - For slowing the exhale and supporting vagal tone during moments of activation.
  • YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link) - For providing a sense of containment and safety to the nervous system.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
